text: During the laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair, the surgeon dissects the preperitoneal space to prepare for the placement of a synthetic mesh.
In this video, we present a technique of dissecting these tissues, allowing to maintain full control of the dissected structures.
The video shows a small medial direct inguinal hernia in a male patient, highlighting the anatomical location and size of the hernia.

The surgeon identifies crucial anatomical structures before incising the peritoneum, including the external iliac artery and vein, the obliquely running vas deferens, and the testicular veins at the deep inguinal ring. The position and filling of the bladder are also assessed.
The assisting surgeon sets the horizon and frames the image, preparing for the peritoneal incision in the laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon grasps a thin layer of the peritoneal lamina approximately five to six centimeters laterally from the deep inguinal ring. After retracting the lamina, a gentle incision is made, allowing gas to enter the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon applies a stronger pull on the peritoneal lamina, causing the insufflated gas to separate the tissues within the avascular layer.
The surgeon identifies small vessels within the peritoneal lamina and uses gentle cauterization to close them before proceeding with the incision.
The surgeon pulls the peritoneum away from the abdominal wall, stretching it taut along the cutting line to facilitate incision.

The surgeon detaches loose connective tissue from the peritoneum while carefully directing the incision. The incision initially moves towards the top of the screen, then slightly medial, and finally cranially just before reaching the medial umbilical ligament.

The surgeon carefully incises the peritoneum while maintaining awareness of the inferior epigastric vessels. The peritoneum is pulled to one side, and the vessels are slid to the other side, ensuring they remain on the rectus muscle without risk of damage.

The surgeon carefully incises the peritoneum while maintaining a safe distance of at least five to six centimeters from the edge of the hernia defect, ensuring the incision follows the correct direction.
The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to apply solid traction on the peritoneum at a right angle, facilitating tissue dissection during the hernia repair procedure.

In the lateral part of the dissected space, the surgeon removes connective and fatty tissue from the peritoneum, leaving it attached to the abdominal wall muscles. In the medial part, the surgeon employs the opposite technique, detaching the tissue from the abdominal wall and leaving it on the peritoneum.
The surgeon positions the bare muscle at the top of the screen while ensuring the fatty tissue is directed downward towards the bladder.

The surgeon continues dissection in the current layer, revealing the pubic symphysis as a clear, white, dense structure oriented transversely, serving as a major anatomical landmark in the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon dissects adjacent tissues to expose Cooper's ligament, revealing the ligamentous and fascial tissues covering the upper edge of the pubic bone.
The surgeon carefully dissects the space just above the anterior wall of the bladder, taking precautions to avoid puncture damage and thermal injuries to the bladder tissue.

The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to bluntly press down on the bladder, stretching the tissue fibers between the bladder wall and the pubic symphysis. These fibers are then carefully cut under full visual inspection.

The video shows a small subtle protrusion of the pubic symphysis, identified as the center of the symphysis. The surgeon uses this landmark to guide dissection approximately two centimeters towards the opposite side.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the hernial sac and internal ring after completing the initial dissection of the lateral Hasselbach space and medial Retzius space.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to carefully cut and isolate individual connective tissue fibers between the peritoneum and the abdominal wall, ensuring precise separation of these structures.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to avoid damaging the parietal peritoneum during dissection, ensuring that the peritoneum remains intact for later suturing without requiring additional closure of defects.
The surgeon slides tissues from the peritoneum towards the abdominal wall, using an instrument in one hand to stretch the lower flap of the peritoneum, facilitating this maneuver.
The surgeon dissects and separates the peritoneum in the area of the inguinal ring, focusing on a medial direct hernia, which appears visually easier to manage compared to a lateral oblique hernia.
The surgeon uses a cutting instrument to sever the stretched tissues within the thin layer of the pre-transversalis fascia, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon releases the peritoneum from the vas deferens, which is situated just below, making the dissection of the peritoneal flap easier.
The surgeon separates the contents of a direct hernia, which has become easier to manage following previous dissection and manipulation of the peritoneum and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon applies solid traction behind the fatty tissue penetrating towards the hernia defect, revealing the boundaries of the defect and the separated tissues at the site.
The surgeon gradually releases successive layers of adipose tissue to reveal the white compact fibrous connective tissue that constitutes the true hernia defect ring.
The surgeon applies traction to the adipose tissue in the area of the hernia defect, revealing a muff-like structure with a narrow opening filled with adipose tissue.
The surgeon releases tissues surrounding the hernia defect, gradually revealing the complete circumference of the defect for better visualization.
The surgeon carefully releases additional adipose tissue adhesions on the lower edge of the hernia defect, maintaining control over the network of small vessels visible in the area.
The surgeon isolates and coagulates vessels to limit further dissection, revealing the full extent of Cooper's ligament.
This material is a presentation of a full recording of this procedure, and only the short parts with cleaning the optics lens have been removed from the video.
The playback rate is one point five times faster than an original speed of the procedure, so you can see how much time is actually needed for the following steps of this surgery.
The surgeon carefully separates tissues on the lateral edge of the direct hernia, taking care to avoid damaging the nearby inferior epigastric vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon exposes the entire perimeter of the abdominal wall defect within the medial hernia, along with the cleansed branch of the pubic bone. The tissues between the bladder and the external iliac vein are separated to prepare for placing the lower edge of the mesh securely without risk of folding.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the lower peritoneal flap in its lateral part, creating sufficient space for the placement of the lower edge of the mesh, ensuring it remains flat while closing the peritoneum.
The surgeon exposes the testicular vessels, which are covered by the peritoneum, in the surgical field.
The surgeon gently slides the testicular vessels away from the peritoneum, effectively separating them without causing damage.
The surgeon sharply cuts a thin layer of pretransverse fascia near the vas deferens, releasing and allowing further separation of the vas deferens from surrounding tissues.

Please note that although we present you with a complete recording of this operation and the progression of the surgery so far actually lasted twelve minutes, the surgeon still uses only the same two instruments and has not changed them and only the grasper and tip of scissors were sufficient to separate the tissues.

However, it should be emphasized that, especially in the case of poorly dissecting tissues and in the case of lateral hernias, there is often a need for additional grasper or dissector, and the separation of tissues is then more often done bluntly.
One should also make sure that the medial space is properly dissected.

The surgeon navigates the area where perivascular adipose tissue containing lymph nodes is visually indistinct from pre-bladder adipose tissue, attempting to separate these layers at an appropriate level despite the uncertainty in demarcation.

The surgeon grasps the pre-bladder fatty tissue and pulls it medially away from the external iliac vein, identifying the thinnest area of adipose tissue for separation. Cauterization is applied to fibers before cutting to prevent fluid accumulation from small lymph vessels. This maneuver is repeated, progressively freeing the space.
The surgeon releases the upper flap of the peritoneum, creating a small pocket intended to secure the superior edge of the synthetic mesh during the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon uses two instruments to grasp the peritoneum, pulling the flap tangentially to the abdominal cavity surface to separate it from the abdominal wall. The flap is gradually moved medially, passing it from one instrument to the other.
The surgeon inserts an appropriate synthetic mesh into the dissected preperitoneal pocket, positioning it within the space created during previous dissection steps.
The surgeon inserts a lightweight three-dimensional polypropylene mesh into the narrow orifice of a direct hernia, ensuring proper placement within the preperitoneal pocket.

The surgeon positions the lower edge of the polypropylene mesh precisely to ensure it maintains appropriate margins around the hernia defect. Care is taken to prevent the mesh from folding due to the peritoneum or urinary bladder movements.

The surgeon lifts the lower flap of the peritoneum upwards, ensuring that the mesh remains stable and does not shift. The hernial sac peritoneum is carefully pulled out to prevent internal strangulation.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using an absorbable suture, completing the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon desquamates gas from the preperitoneal space to ensure the mesh remains in its correct position, preventing any potential shifting.
Thank you for watching.
